model_output_raw_text:
{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = "adamw"","old":"OPTIMIZER = "adam"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"build_scheduler","new":"","old":"","op":"replace_function","source":"def build_scheduler(optimizer: optim.Optimizer, steps_per_epoch: int):
    if not USE_LR_SCHEDULE:
        return None
    total_steps = TRAIN_MAX_STEPS if TRAIN_MAX_STEPS is not None else steps_per_epoch * 20
    total_steps = max(int(total_steps), 1)
    return optim.lr_scheduler.OneCycleLR(
        optimizer,
        max_lr=LEARNING_RATE,
        total_steps=total_steps,
        pct_start=0.1,
        anneal_strategy="cos",
        div_factor=10.0,
        final_div_factor=100.0,
    )","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"With only 256 training steps, the baseline likely benefits more from aggressive early optimization than from a long-horizon constant schedule. This keeps the model unchanged and uses a short-budget OneCycle schedule with AdamW and a higher peak learning rate to improve convergence within the fixed step limit.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current optimization setup is already much stronger, so the next likely gain is a slightly better inductive bias. Adding one more downsampling block and modestly widening the stem should give the model more useful hierarchical features within roughly the same parameter budget, which is a good tradeoff for CIFAR-10 under a short fixed-step run.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model is now strong enough that a low-complexity regularization tweak is a sensible next move. Label smoothing often improves CIFAR-10 validation cross-entropy directly, especially with AdamW and a short OneCycle run, while leaving the rest of the training setup unchanged.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Label smoothing moved validation loss in the wrong direction, so the next clean test is to remove it and use a larger batch. With a fixed 256-step budget, doubling batch size lets the model see substantially more examples while preserving the current architecture and optimizer setup.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move to batch size 128 produced a large gain, which suggests the current setup may still be slightly underpowered in optimization scale. Increasing the OneCycle peak learning rate is the simplest next test and is well aligned with the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last learning-rate increase still helped, so the current configuration may not yet have reached its best optimization scale. A further step up in OneCycle peak learning rate is the highest-signal follow-up before spending complexity on architecture changes.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization scale is already strong, so the next simple place to add capacity is the MLP head. The current head is a fairly tight bottleneck relative to the deeper feature extractor, and widening it should improve class separation with only a modest compute increase.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last head-capacity increase helped materially, which suggests the model is still capacity-limited rather than overfitting. A modest widening of the convolutional trunk is the cleanest next step to strengthen the learned features without changing the training recipe.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Head widening produced a strong gain earlier, and the more recent trunk widening still helped, so the model likely remains capacity-limited. Increasing the classifier width again is a low-risk way to add representational power with much less runtime cost than another trunk expansion.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        pct_start=0.2,","old":"        pct_start=0.1,","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The extra head width did not help, so reverting that change and reallocating budget within the same model is a better direction. A longer OneCycle warmup/ramp should suit the larger batch and wider trunk better than peaking extremely early in a 256-step run.","secondary_modes":["layout","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The longer OneCycle ramp improved results, which usually means the optimization is tolerating more aggressive scheduling. Raising the peak learning rate is the simplest way to exploit that without adding architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["summaries","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"At this point the model is training under a very short budget with a relatively aggressive schedule, so the current weight decay may be slightly constraining feature growth. Reducing regularization is a simple next test that often helps short-run AdamW training once the architecture and learning-rate scale are already strong.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        pct_start=0.25,","old":"        pct_start=0.2,","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"Lowering weight decay did not help, so that change should be reverted. The next clean refinement is to keep the stronger regularization and shift a bit more of the short 256-step budget into the OneCycle ramp, which may better match the larger batch and wider network.","secondary_modes":["caching","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        pct_start=0.2,","old":"        pct_start=0.25,","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Pushing the ramp longer did not help. The largest remaining simple lever is data coverage: with only 256 optimizer steps, increasing batch size further lets the model see much closer to a full pass over the 50k training set while keeping the otherwise strong schedule and architecture intact.","secondary_modes":["summaries","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump from batch 128 to 192 produced the largest recent gain, which strongly suggests this workload is dominated by data coverage within the 256-step cap. Increasing batch size again is the simplest way to push even closer to a full effective pass over the training set while preserving the now-strong architecture and schedule.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 6e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With batch size now at 256, the strongest remaining low-complexity lever is optimization scale. The recent gains suggest the run is benefiting from larger effective batches, so a modest increase in OneCycle peak learning rate is the most direct follow-up.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 6e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The higher peak learning rate hurt, so that should be reverted. The strongest remaining lever is still data coverage per optimizer step: moving from batch 256 to 320 increases the amount of unique training signal consumed within the fixed 256-step budget without changing the rest of the recipe.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last batch-size increase still improved validation loss, so the dominant effect remains better data coverage per optimizer step. With only a few steps left, the highest-value move is to continue pushing batch size upward rather than introducing a new source of complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last several improvements have come from increasing batch size, and the effect is still clearly positive. With only two steps left, the highest-expected-value move is to push data throughput further and let each of the fixed 256 updates aggregate even more training signal.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 640","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The most reliable gains in the late run have come from steadily increasing batch size, and that effect remained strong even at 512. With one step left, the highest-expected-value move is to continue that trend with a moderate further increase rather than change the now-stable optimizer or architecture.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}
